summary: Gruber defends Apple's philosophy of being 'first to do it right' rather than 'first to do it,' using FaceTime vs. Android video calling as the central example. He contrasts the iPhone 4's seamless, built-in video calling experience with the HTC Evo's reliance on buggy third-party apps like Qik and Fring. Citing David Pogue's frustrating experience with Evo video calls, Gruber argues that including hardware without polished software doesn't count as delivering a feature. He frames this as a fundamental philosophical divide: Apple holds features until they're fully baked, while Android ships hardware and lets third-party developers figure out the experience. He acknowledges this same divide will be read differently by each camp's fans, but clearly sides with the integrated approach.
key_points: Apple's strategy is to delay features until the full hardware-software experience meets their quality bar, not because they can't ship sooner
FaceTime required no account creation, no third-party apps, and worked like a regular phone call — a fundamentally different user experience than Qik/Fring on the Evo
David Pogue's real-world testing of Evo video calling revealed it was essentially broken despite the hardware being present
The 'normal person' test is the real benchmark: give non-technical users both phones and see who can actually make a video call
The Apple vs. Android divide maps to a deeper philosophical split about whether platforms should ship hardware first and let developers fill gaps, or ship integrated experiences
money_quotes: This is the heart of the disconnect. Those who see these claims as 'absolute bullshit' are only going to see Apple as getting worse over the next few years.
Apple isn't going to include a hardware feature just for the sake of having it. They only include hardware for which they have compelling software to complete the experience.
Take some normal people, where by 'normal' I mean people who have never heard of TechCrunch or Daring Fireball.
There is no such thing as a 'FaceTime account' you need to create or log in to. It doesn't require the installation of any third-party apps.
Do you include the half-baked stuff, or hold it until it's fully-baked?

Apple wasn't going to include a front-facing camera until they had software that made it useful in an iPhone-caliber way. HTC is happy to include a front-facing camera and leave its utility in the hands of third-party developers.
Android fans will read it and say, 'Exactly — give us the hardware and let developers figure out what to do with it.' iPhone fans will read it and say, 'I can't wait to get an iPhone 4.'
key_insight: Having the hardware capability is meaningless without polished software to make it usable — shipping a front-facing camera without great video calling is shipping a checklist item, not a feature.
